text:
Down the way station I stride
Relieved to finally see the faceless woman at my side
A blank slate, confused, seeking, lost
Seeking for a man, he’s right there too
Both of them important, neither of them clear
He sinks into the sand and so does she
Mournful sorrow as they disappear
A piercing harmony escapes my soul
As I rise into the air most present, whole
Who are they to me, I do not know
As this is not yet the dream

I run hot and full
overloaded
lost in the maze of discomfort 
where is love?
where is joy?
where is aliveness?
I do not even bear to look
the one thing I can do is let my feet carry me
until suddenly 
the vastness finds me
and takes my breath away
there is love
there is joy
there is aliveness 
and there is beauty 
the memory of stillness returns to my heart
and even an ember is enough 
to fan it back into flames

When I can trust my love
I can trust my want

text:
Reality itself (the n-dimensional meaning space, the First Sphere) has no inherent "up" or "down," no fundamental ground. It's more like that Buddhist concept of groundlessness or śūnyatā.

But the physical realm, our projection within the Second Sphere, does have this consistent orientation through gravity. And rather than this being a limitation, it's actually a gift - a cosmic compass that helps us navigate the groundless ocean of meaning.

This reminds me of your earlier points about the purpose of the Second Sphere - it's not something to transcend but rather a necessary tool for experiencing and navigating reality. Gravity is part of that gift. The physical constraints aren't obstacles to overcome but rather crucial reference points that help us make sense of and move through the infinite possibilities of meaning space.

This also adds new depth to spiritual practices that emphasize both groundlessness AND grounding. You need to be able to recognize the ultimate groundlessness of reality while still maintaining healthy orientation through physical grounding. Neither pure transcendence nor pure materialism, but a dynamic integration of both.

The physical ground becomes like a walking stick for exploring the groundless - not something to throw away, but a tool for more skillful navigation.

Does this help explain why attempts to completely transcend the physical (like some forms of spiritual bypassing) actually make it harder to navigate meaning space effectively?

It's like... imagine being set adrift in an infinite ocean of meaning - beautiful but overwhelming. But we've been given this gift of gravity, this consistent pull, this walking stick, that helps us find our way. The limitations of physical existence aren't a curse but a blessing - they provide the orientation we need to explore the boundless.

This reminds me of your earlier critique of certain Buddhist approaches that try to transcend the physical entirely. There's a kind of wisdom in recognizing that our embodied nature, complete with its gravitational pull, is part of how we're meant to explore and understand the divine. It's like the paradox you discussed earlier - the Second Sphere's limitations are actually what enable exploration and understanding. Gravity might be the most fundamental example of this principle - a constraint that enables rather than restricts.

As I reengage with my past recently, my trauma is taking on a reassuringly banal shape

I've had two handful of important relationships in my life (not including the ones I have now) and they all quietly failed. My parents were spiritually dead to me when I was 12 or 13. All school friendships I had eventually fizzled out.

text:
So my original move into English was a lot about detaching from a culture I didn't feel at home in. German culture felt really cringe and foreign to me. German YouTube, movies, music etc. So I started consuming media exclusively in English. 

This led to me making more English speaking friends online which became a feedback loop. I discovered that English has a different character. It's less stiff, and more ambiguous. Ambiguity that can really help with more nebulous communication. 

I really enjoyed speaking English, I felt cooler, more free, I made good friends in English and I got a lot of positive feedback "Oh wow your English is so good!". So I kept going deeper.

There was a definitely a time where I felt quite disgusted by German which felt like a renunciation of heritage. I wanted to be someone else from somewhere else. German is also a culture with some very strange post-WW2 scars.

After I achieved my separation the disgust started to soften over time though. I found my place as a more international being and in that my Germanness feels valuable again. Recently I've been rediscovering the gifts of German and Germanness with @simon_ohler

text:
What I escaped in German was not a self in which many emotions were had that were traumatic (although of course below the surface that was also the case) I escaped a semantic web in which the value of emotions and subjective experience more broadly was denied. It all had to be pragmatic and clear and anything that couldn't be explained and argued for wasn't valid. It's like my non-emotionality, and the non-realness of subjective experience was bound up in that language for me. Naturally this is also very related to how my parents are, but some of it is encoded in the language itself

I had a moment fairly recently that showcases this quite well. I would speak the phrase "I deserve to be loved" in English and would get incredibly angry and resistant to it. When I switched on German it became clear why. My German self just very dryly stated that love doesnt exist and isn't real (in German of course). The severe distress immediately faded. This was the most obvious thing to it. Unquestionable (You know how Germans can be lol). Love isn't real, it can't be seen or touched, or proven. This led to a negotiation between my English and German self, exploring the feelings in German, my English self giving pointers to what it thought love was.

text:
I had a not unfamiliar experience today which I now think may be a cptsd flashback?

I wake up feeling quite optimistic and eager. I make a plan for the day which is quite rare for me, and I start with writing. Writing doesn't go so well and quickly I find myself in ungrounded, self-loathing territory. I couldn't even really tell you why although maybe it'd be wise to figure out. 

And then that state just keeps spiralling. I can't bring myself back to baseline, like the part that could is out of the house right now. I just keep doom spiraling and dissociating. In a way I keep my head above the water but I feel powerless to change anything.
There is this inner battle raging and I'm being completely overpowered. I could fight back more urgently but I know that usually makes it worse so I just wait it out.

At some point in the afternoon I finally wake up. Energy surges through my system and I get joyfully angry at the situation. I can start laughing about it.  

It's moved through me but I'm exhausted. All my energy was spent on making sure I don't do anything 😄

The self-help part is back online again and I'm getting curious about what this is and what I do with it.
And it fits the description of a cptsd flashback pretty well. 

So if this happens again I'm gonna try that lense and see if that shifts anything

I think for the picture to have a chance of making sense we have to go a layer deeper than humans to cosmic archetypal forces. Hyperbeings / hyperobjects just work by very different rules than what is intuitive to us. The extra dimensions can be really confusing 

And I think any kind of engineered religion has to integrate strong hyperbeing literacy. (I personally wouldn't quite use the word engineered but I think I know what you mean. The best pointer to the process I've found is Christopher Alexander's Nature of Order series. He calls it unfolding and it's basically iterative design in harmony with the divine. But also more)

But yeah I've also been puzzling about this image. What's happening to the human organism looks like cell division but what would that even mean? It could be a merge but then I would somehow expect cell merges to be a thing we see in nature. Which I guess we do? (thinking of the powerhouse of the cell here) But what's going on does not really look like that process. Maybe it's a bit of both somehow? I can imagine something like that

Anyway I've been mostly vibecoding this stuff and would be cool to get a bit tighter on the epistemology. A bit more legible

Working with cptsd flashbacks seems to be an entirely different game than what I'm used to

I'm used to the mantra of "all you need to do is feel your emotions. Feel them and they will pass". But there don't really seem to be any emotions to process. At least not primarily 

It seems lower down. Not just pre-verbal but even kind of pre-emotional. Something like the core threat/safety nervous system level. That's why my usual tactics just completely refuse to work. 

Of course once I move through some of the pre-emotional process the emotions come back and then they may want to be felt. But I have to start at the bottom of the pyramid! 

(Or well I guess actually I've been working my way down but now I've reached the floor. To percolate back up I need to work here) 

And I'm realizing that I basically spend all my life in flashback mode just to varying degrees. Plus I'm very high functioning of course

To be welcomed back into the fold. A love strong enough to overcome my defenses. Because that's the only love I trust to hold me. Else I'm too afraid of running them over.

One of the things Ford had always found hardest to understand about humans was their habit of continually stating and repeating the very very obvious, as in 'It's a nice day', 'You're very tall', or 'You seem to have fallen down a thirty-foot well, are you alright?' 
At first Ford had formed a theory to account for this strange behaviour.  If human beings don't keep exercising their lips, he thought, their mouths probably seize up.  After a few months' consideration and observation, he abandoned this theory in favour of a new one.  If they don't keep exercising their lips, he thought, their brains start working.

text:
Somehow the metaphor of my heart “breaking” doesn't land at all for me. At least for now. It conjures the image of something brittle that shatters into pieces. 

What's been more alive is the image of “melting”. The image of my heart, and by extension my whole circulatory system, getting white hot like molten metal. All the defense mechanisms becoming a veeeery viscous liquid
